texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) is an emerging thermal separation technique, using a hydrophobic microporous membrane. Initially, the process used MF membranes with a hydrophobic character, such as PTFE and PVDF membranes. These membranes are not wetted by aqueous feed liquids and their microporous structure allows the transport of water vapor molecules through the membrane due to an applied temperature difference. On average 23% of the publications on MD focus on membrane engineering. Besides the traditional stretching and phase inversion technique, many novel techniques are proposed, including electrospinning, the use of carbon nanotubes, surface modification techniques and the design of novel polymers. This article provides guidelines for the evaluation of these membranes measured using a large variety in process conditions at lab scale. In the second part, an overview of the synthesis methods used for MD was given and the advantages, disadvantages and the performance of the respective membrane types in MD were evaluated.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) is an emerging membrane technology. The process uses a hydrophobic microporous membrane, separating the liquid phase from the vapor phase within the membrane pores. Vapors are able to pass through the membrane, whereas the liquid feed including dissolved components, is retained by the membrane. This enables the use of MD as a technique for separation of non-volatile solutes from water, where water is transported through the membrane. Additionally, MD can be used for removal of volatile organic compounds from water, where the volatile organic compounds are transferred through the membrane pores more easily compared to the less volatile water molecules.
texts.text: Membrane distillation (MD) is an emerging membrane technology. The process uses a hydrophobic microporous membrane, separating the liquid phase from the vapor phase within the membrane pores. Vapors are able to pass through the membrane, whereas the liquid feed including dissolved components, is retained by the membrane. This enables the use of MD as a technique for separation of non-volatile solutes from water, where water is transported through the membrane. Additionally, MD can be used for removal of volatile organic compounds from water, where the volatile organic compounds are transferred through the membrane pores more easily compared to the less volatile water molecules.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hydrophobic microfiltration (MF) membranes are widely used in the MD field. However, these membranes are not specifically optimized for MD, although such optimization is expected to severely improve the process performance [1-6]. The research interest in MD in general is exponentially increasing. On average 23% of over 100 articles yearly published on MD focuses on membrane engineering in particular [7], resulting in many novel approaches for membrane production.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, a literature review is given on the different methods used to produce MD membranes. In the first part, a brief overview is given of basic principle, the evaluation parameters and membrane and module types used for MD. The traditional membrane synthesis techniques used to produce microporous membranes from intrinsically hydrophobic polymers, namely stretching and phase inversion are discussed in the second part. Finally, novel and emerging synthesis techniques are described, including electrospinning, carbon nanotubes and the use of surface modifications to tune and improve the properties of the membrane surface. For all these membranes, the performance in MD is assessed and the benefits and drawbacks of the most interesting membrane fabrication methods are given.

texts.text: 2. MD basics

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Basic principle
texts.text: 2.1. Basic principle

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the most simplified MD operation, a hot liquid feed solution and a cold permeate solution sustain a temperature difference and hence a vapor pressure difference over the membrane. The mass transport is initiated by evaporation of the feed liquid at the phase boundary between vapor and liquid at the membrane pores (1). Secondly, the vapor pressure gradient induces diffusion of the vapor molecules through the membrane (2), after which condensation (3) occurs at permeate side (Fig. 1). The vapor transport through the membrane pores occurs through Knudsen diffusion, molecular diffusion or Poisseuille type of flow depending on the membrane pore size, the mean free path of the evaporated molecules and the presence of trapped air in the pores [3,7,8]. Simultaneously, heat is transported through the membrane due to the flux and to conduction, the latter considered as energy loss. More information on mass and heat transport can be found elsewhere [3,712].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some critical notes should be made on the method of evaluating membranes for MD at lab scale. Most membranes are measured using relatively low salt concentrations and high driving forces. Temperature differences of 50-60 ° C are no rarity in lab scale experiments, whereas temperature differences between 5 and 15 ° C are achieved at pilot scale [23,24]. Recently it has been shown that at high salinity, the flux of thin membranes (e.g., supported PTFE) is strongly reduced. The effect of salinity is even more pronounced at lower driving force, i.e. low temperature differences occurring at pilot scale. Nevertheless, for lab scale experiments often unrealistically high temperature differences are applied [24,25]. The effect of salinity on the membrane in real applications is therefore strongly underestimated at lab scale when using unrealistically high driving forces.

Temperature differences of 50-60 ° C are no rarity in lab scale experiments, whereas temperature differences between 5 and 15 ° C are achieved at pilot scale [23,24]. Recently it has been shown that at high salinity, the flux of thin membranes (e.g., supported PTFE) is strongly reduced. The effect of salinity is even more pronounced at lower driving force, i.e. low temperature differences occurring at pilot scale. Nevertheless, for lab scale experiments often unrealistically high temperature differences are applied [24,25]. The effect of salinity on the membrane in real applications is therefore strongly underestimated at lab scale when using unrealistically high driving forces.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Secondly, the advantage of using membranes with low thermal conductivity and high energy efficiency at pilot scale is strongly underestimated at lab scale for two reasons.
texts.text: Secondly, the advantage of using membranes with low thermal conductivity and high energy efficiency at pilot scale is strongly underestimated at lab scale for two reasons.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first reason is that at lab scale, flow velocities of 0.05-0.5 m/ s are commonly used [26,27], whereas at pilot scale the flow velocity is limited by the pressure drop over the module and varies from 0.01 to 0.05 m/s [28,29]. Therefore, the mixing and hence polarization phenomena are more pronounced at pilot scale. Since less temperature polarization is observed for less conductive membranes, these membranes will show a higher flux. This effect is underestimated at lab scale because of the improved mixing.
texts.text: The first reason is that at lab scale, flow velocities of 0.05-0.5 m/ s are commonly used [26,27], whereas at pilot scale the flow velocity is limited by the pressure drop over the module and varies from 0.01 to 0.05 m/s [28,29]. Therefore, the mixing and hence polarization phenomena are more pronounced at pilot scale. Since less temperature polarization is observed for less conductive membranes, these membranes will show a higher flux. This effect is underestimated at lab scale because of the improved mixing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The second reason is the difference in inflow energy per m 2 membrane between lab and pilot scale [30]. As an example, for a lab scale experiment with feed temperature 60 ° C and 45 ° C, flow rate of 60 kg/h and module dimensions of 0.0108 m 2 , the inflow energy equals 92.7 kW/m 2 , whereas only 14% of the energy is exchanged in the module. At pilot scale, however, using feed and permeate temperature of 70 and 20 ° C and a flow rate of 1000 kg/h for a membrane area of 7.2 m 2 , the inflow energy equals only 8.1 kW/m 2 and 82% of this energy is exchanged in module. Both at lab and pilot scale, the fraction of energy that is lost due to conduction cannot contribute to flux. At lab scale sufficient energy is left to sustain the driving force over the membrane. Therefore, the efficiency of the process does affect the flux only to a minor extent (by increasing the temperature polarization effects). In contrast, at pilot scale almost all energy is consumed by the module. Therefore, if a lot of heat is exchanged due to losses, less energy is left for evaporation, i.e. flux. In this case, the amount of heat loss through the membrane (i.e., the energy efficiency) can severely affect the flux through the membrane, showing the importance of energy efficiency measurements in the evaluation of membranes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: suitable for MD. The most commonly used membranes in MD include the stretched PTFE and PE membranes and the phase inverted PVDF and PP membranes. Ceramic membranes are sporadically used for MD. Due to their high thermal conductivity a lot of heat is lost through the membrane matrix [31-34]. Additionally, ceramics are often more expensive, while MD requires cheap membranes. Therefore, in this review, only polymeric membranes are considered. As described in Fig. 2, the membrane properties directly affect the membrane performance and therefore, an optimized membrane specifically designed for MD could further improve the process. This section gives a short summary of the effect of the membrane characteristics on the performance in MD and offers a guideline for the development of improved membranes. More detailed information can be found in other review articles on the subject [6,17,21].

Ceramic membranes are sporadically used for MD. Due to their high thermal conductivity a lot of heat is lost through the membrane matrix [31-34]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane must consist of at least one hydrophobic layer, which is not wetted by the feed liquid. As the membrane is a contactor between two phases, non-volatile solutes are 100% retained. The susceptibility to membrane wetting is evaluated based on the liquid entry pressure, which is defined as the pressure at which liquid penetrates through the membrane. To ensure a proper operation under fluctuating pressures and temperatures in the operation plant, a LEP with the feed liquid of minimum 2.5 bar is recommended [35,36]. This liquid entry pressure is affected by the contact angle with the fluid. A water contact angle above 90 ° is required for the treatment of salty aqueous solutions with MD. In the presence of surface active compounds, e.g. alcohols, surfactants or oils, the membrane might benefit from an oleophobic surface [37-40]. Also a smaller maximum pore diameter affect the LEP in a positive way [41,42], while the average pore diameter correlates with flux, depending on the mass transfer mechanism. In general a pore diameter between 0.05 and 1 m m is recommended [6,35]. However, for an average pore diameter larger than 0.3 l m, little incentive from the DCMD flux is gained. Therefore, a small pore distribution, with 0.3 m m as average pore diameter is recommended for MD [22]. The porosity is limited by the mechanical strength requirement membrane, but is considered as the most important membrane parameter by many authors [35,43,44]. The flux is linearly proportional with porosity, while a lower thermal conductivity of the membrane is also obtained for more porous membranes. Less heat loss due to conduction through the membrane causes higher energy efficiency, but also an improved flux. Less temperature polarization occurs and more driving force is available for flux. Hence, also the conductivity of the membrane matrix affects performance. For polymers, the thermal conductivity varies from 0.1 up to 0.5 W m � 1 K � 1 ). Only a few studies focus on ceramic membranes for MD with improved stability, but also higher thermal conductivity above 1 W m � 1 K � 1 [31,33,34,45,46].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Different opinions exist on the effect of membrane thickness. Numerous studies state that flux is decreasing with increasing membrane thickness [35,41,43,47-51]. Some authors recently focused on the calculation of an optimal membrane thickness [24,25,51-54]. Mass transfer is improved by reducing the membrane thickness, while also more heat loss reduces the driving force. Different authors indicate an optimal membrane thickness between 10 and 60 m m for seawater desalination in lab scale conditions (i.e., high temperature difference and high flow velocities) [51-54]. For full scale hollow fibers, Ali et al. also reports an optimal thickness of 20-400 m m, depending on the fiber length and thermal conductivity of the membrane [55]. For spiral wound DCMD, the optimal thickness ranges from 41 to 146 m mdepending on the salt concentration and the availability and cost of the waste heat [24]. This broad range of recommended membrane thicknesses exist from the fact that this optimal membrane thickness is case specific and depends on process conditions, membrane properties and salinity. The recommendation to use a membrane thickness of 10-60 m mis proven to be valid for seawater desalination by different authors. However, MD is currently also focusing on high salinity applications. Thicker isotropic hydrophobic membrane layers of above 100 m mthat are incompressible are required for improving the performance of MD in the case of high salinity applications at full scale. In this case, lower driving forces are applied compared to many lab scale studies and hence the effect of salinity becomes even more pronounced [25]. In some cases an optimal membrane thickness up to 700 m mwas calculated [25].
texts.text: Different opinions exist on the effect of membrane thickness. Numerous studies state that flux is decreasing with increasing membrane thickness [35,41,43,47-51]. Some authors recently focused on the calculation of an optimal membrane thickness [24,25,51-54]. Mass transfer is improved by reducing the membrane thickness, while also more heat loss reduces the driving force. Different authors indicate an optimal membrane thickness between 10 and 60 m m for seawater desalination in lab scale conditions (i.e., high temperature difference and high flow velocities) [51-54]. For full scale hollow fibers, Ali et al. also reports an optimal thickness of 20-400 m m, depending on the fiber length and thermal conductivity of the membrane [55]. For spiral wound DCMD, the optimal thickness ranges from 41 to 146 m mdepending on the salt concentration and the availability and cost of the waste heat [24]. This broad range of recommended membrane thicknesses exist from the fact that this optimal membrane thickness is case specific and depends on process conditions, membrane properties and salinity. The recommendation to use a membrane thickness of 10-60 m mis proven to be valid for seawater desalination by different authors. However, MD is currently also focusing on high salinity applications. Thicker isotropic hydrophobic membrane layers of above 100 m mthat are incompressible are required for improving the performance of MD in the case of high salinity applications at full scale. In this case, lower driving forces are applied compared to many lab scale studies and hence the effect of salinity becomes even more pronounced [25]. In some cases an optimal membrane thickness up to 700 m mwas calculated [25].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Moreover, it is also described that membrane compaction might be problematic at pilot scale operations [18,19,56,57]. It was found that stretched PE and PTFE used in commercial MD modules compacts with 20-30% at 0.3 bar, which is the operational regime of the modules [19,56]. Both studies show that membrane compaction can result in a severe reduction of the flux and process efficiency. Another major challenge in membrane production is the fabrication of large amounts of membrane surfaces without defects, the latter causing a reduction of the salt retention. An overview of the membrane properties of the membranes currently used for MD and the recommended values based on different studies focusing on the subject are given in Table 1.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.5. Membrane configurations
texts.text: 2.5. Membrane configurations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most commonly used membrane configurations are the flat sheet membranes, which can be placed both in plate and frame or in spiral wound modules, visualized in Fig. 4A and B. The plate and frame modules allow for high specific flow rates, which can increase the production rate. Additionally, the membranes can be replaced more easily. On the contrary, these modules have a low packing density (100-400 m 2 /m 3 ) and a poor energy efficiency [71,72]. Spiral wound modules have the advantage of a higher packing density (300-1000 m 2 /m 3 ) and have an increased energy efficiency compared to the plate and frame modules. Alternatively, hollow fiber membranes can also be used. The packing density can then even be increased up to 9000 m 2 /m 3 . However, these modules are lacking turbulence promotors and hence, mixing is relatively poor and more pronounced thermal boundary layers are formed. These modules also suffer more from fouling, reducing or even blocking the inner diameter of the fibers.

texts.orig: 3. Traditional membrane production methods for hydrophobic membranes
texts.text: 3. Traditional membrane production methods for hydrophobic membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As described in the literature [13,75,76], polymeric porous membranes are traditionally produced by stretching, phase inversion, sintering, track-etching or template leaching. The last three methods results in membranes with relatively low porosity. However, with the phase inversion and stretching method high porosities can be obtained and therefore, these methods are found suitable to produce the required membrane structure for MD as given in Table 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The stretching process can be applied for semi-crystalline polymer materials like PTFE and PE and is a solvent free technique. The polymer is heated above the melting point and extruded in the desired morphology. The stretching perpendicular to the direction of the extrusion provides the porosity of the membrane visualized in Fig. 5.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Phase inversion
texts.text: 3.2. Phase inversion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Isotropic phase inverted PP, PVDF and modified PES membranes are commercially available and suitable for MD. The phase inversion process is based on a transition between two phases, induced by a change of polymer solubility. Starting from a homogeneous mixture, i.e. the dope, a change in composition or conditions induces demixing of the mixture into a polymer rich and a polymer poor phase. Upon further separation, the solubility of the polymer is reduced and a solid phase with specific morphology will be formed. The phase separation and precipitation can be induced in different ways (Fig. 6):
texts.text: Isotropic phase inverted PP, PVDF and modified PES membranes are commercially available and suitable for MD. The phase inversion process is based on a transition between two phases, induced by a change of polymer solubility. Starting from a homogeneous mixture, i.e. the dope, a change in composition or conditions induces demixing of the mixture into a polymer rich and a polymer poor phase. Upon further separation, the solubility of the polymer is reduced and a solid phase with specific morphology will be formed. The phase separation and precipitation can be induced in different ways (Fig. 6):

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: - Non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS): the casting solution is immersed in a coagulation bath containing non-solvent, resulting in solvent-nonsolvent exchange.
texts.text: Non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS): the casting solution is immersed in a coagulation bath containing non-solvent, resulting in solvent-nonsolvent exchange.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: - Vapor induced phase separation (VIPS): the casting solution takes up the non-solvent from the vapor phase.
texts.text: Vapor induced phase separation (VIPS): the casting solution takes up the non-solvent from the vapor phase.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: - Temperature induced phase separation (TIPS): a decrease in temperature induces the precipitation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besides the membrane synthesized in the more traditional way, recently many novel production techniques are explored to improve the membrane performance in MD. Mostly, the efforts in membrane engineering for MD aim to provide high flux membranes. This section provides the reader with benefits, potential and drawbacks of each technology.
texts.text: Besides the membrane synthesized in the more traditional way, recently many novel production techniques are explored to improve the membrane performance in MD. Mostly, the efforts in membrane engineering for MD aim to provide high flux membranes. This section provides the reader with benefits, potential and drawbacks of each technology.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Electrospinning
texts.text: 4.1. Electrospinning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Electrospinning allows to produce a unique class of nanomaterials with interesting properties for membrane technology called nanofibers [83]. Electrospun membranes show a high porosity above 80%, with interconnected open pore structures and a high surface roughness, as visualized in Fig. 8. These properties result in high flux and thermally efficient membranes [60,84,85]. The membranes also have a high strength-to-weight ratio and can be produced with thicknesses ranging from 48 up to 1529 m m[50,86].
texts.text: Electrospinning allows to produce a unique class of nanomaterials with interesting properties for membrane technology called nanofibers [83]. Electrospun membranes show a high porosity above 80%, with interconnected open pore structures and a high surface roughness, as visualized in Fig. 8. These properties result in high flux and thermally efficient membranes [60,84,85]. The membranes also have a high strength-to-weight ratio and can be produced with thicknesses ranging from 48 up to 1529 m m[50,86].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The electrospinneret producing the nanofibers contains a metallic syringe, a high voltage power supply and a collector, as visualized in Fig. 9. The syringe is filled with a polymer solution or by a melted polymer. The syringe pump drives the liquid to the needle tip forming a droplet, which is exposed to the electric field. When the Coulomb forces overcome the surface tension, a so called fiber jet is ejected. The solvent evaporates from the fiber jet and a solid polymer fiber is deposited randomly on the collector forming a fibrous structure. Different materials are already explored in the literature, including PVDF [50,86,87,89], PVDF hydrophobic clay [88], PVDF -SiO2 [90], PVDF-co-HFP [91], PVDF-co-HFP - carbon nanotubes [92], PTFE/PVA [93], Matrimid [94] and polystyrene [95,96].

texts.orig: 4.2. Carbon nanotubes
texts.text: 4.2. Carbon nanotubes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are increasingly investigated in several desalination applications, including forward osmosis (FO), MD and capacitive deionization (CDI) [100]. Carbon nanotubes have superior durability and different studies on the transport properties show that the specific hollow structure can improve the transport of vapor molecules. Fig. 10 shows the different mechanisms of transport in MD in the presence of immobilized carbon nanotubes. Carbon nanotubes are highly hydrophobic and therefore reduce the tendency for wetting. Through the membrane pores, the normal transport path of Knudsen or molecular diffusion occurs. Carbon nanotubes are known to have a fast sorption and desorption capacity and therefore can activate the diffusion through the membrane pores. Additionally surface diffusion along the nanotube wall can occur.

texts.orig: 4.3. Surface modification
texts.text: 4.3. Surface modification

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Multiple membrane modification methods were developed to change surface properties. With these techniques hydrophilic membranes can be hydrophobized, making them suitable for MD. In this way hydrophilic polymers can be used as a base material for the production of MD membranes, which are more versatile and easier to tune in the production process. Additionally, surface modification can be useful for improving the surface properties of hydrophobic membranes by producing superhydrophobic or oleophobic membrane surfaces. An overview of the different modification techniques described in literature is given in this section.

Polyamide (PA) with a pore size of 0.45 m m was successfully coated using a hydrophobic styrenebutadiene rubber coating, achieving a salt retention of 99.9% during a five days test [107]. Solvay patented a procedure using a perfluoropolyether polymer (Fluorolink) to coat three different hydrophilic membranes: polyethersulfone (PES), regenerated cellulose (RC) and hydrophilic PVDF. The contact angle for all three membranes exceeded 125 ° and a liquid entry pressure of at least 2.2 bar was achieved [108]. A perfluoropolyether was also used to modify a PVDF hollow fiber membrane by Yang et al. [109]. The fibers were modified using TiO2 precursor sol, which was then fluorinated using 1H, 1H, 2H, 2H-perfluorododecyltrichlorosilane (FTCS). The permeate quality remained constant for the treated membrane, while the virgin membrane shows an increase of the electrical conductivity of the permeate after 2 h. An overview of the achieved flux and membrane permeability is given in Table 4. Only the membranes coated with the Fluorolink coating achieved high flux and high permeability, similar to commercial membranes, with as drawback the relatively low liquid entry pressure of 2.2 bar compared to the standard PTFE membranes.

texts.orig: Tetrafluoromethane was successfully applied using (high) vacuum plasma for modification of flat sheet and hollow fiber hydrophilic polyethersulfone membranes as well, achieving a contact angle of 120 ° [112] and 144 ° [113]. No leakages were observed during DCMD. Gancarz et al. [114] successfully investigated a vacuum plasma coating using perfluorohexane and hexafluorobenzene on a track-etched poly(ethylene terephthalate) membrane. A contact angle between 100 ° and 110 ° was achieved, with DCMD fluxes equal to a supported PTFE membrane and sugar rejection of 98-100% [114]. The company P2i has a patented technology to apply a hydrophobic nanocoating of 1H, 1H, 2H, 2Hperfluorodecyl acrylate under medium vacuum pressure on a PVDF hollow fiber membrane [109,118]. A contact angle of 105 ° , liquid entry pressure of 3.8 bar and a salt retention of 100% over 600 h was achieved. Compared to the original membrane, the pore size decreased from 0.4 to 0.2 l mafter plasma modification, which also results in a flux decrease up to 20% depending on the MD process conditions. Table 4 compares the flux and permeability of these types of membranes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3.3. Surface modifying macromolecules
texts.text: 4.3.3. Surface modifying macromolecules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another method to produce composite membranes is the use of fluorinated surface modifying macromolecules (SMM) in the phase inversion process. These molecules can be mixed with hydrophilic polymers like polyetherimide [115,119] or polysulfone [62]. Dur- ing the solvent evaporation time these SMM's migrate to the membrane surface to minimize the surface tension, forming the thin hydrophobic layer [120,121]. The process is visualized in Fig. 11. As bulk material, PEI and PES are proposed for MD. A hydrophobic layer thickness below 10 m m, a water contact angle of about 100 ° and a relatively low pore size of about 0.02 m m is achieved with this technique. At low salinity these membranes perform slightly better compared to the standard PTFE membranes (Table 4) [122]. However, due to the very low thickness, a sufficiently bulk driving force and mixing must be provided to these membranes to allow for positive fluxes when treating high salinity brines [25].

texts.orig: 4.4. Novel polymers
texts.text: 4.4. Novel polymers

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently also novel hydrophobic polymers have been developed to combine the benefits of the soluble PVDF and the highly hydrophobic PTFE for different applications [123]. Different fluorinated copolymers are being developed with increased solubility in commonly used solvents, while still achieving a high hydrophobicity and tensile strength. These polymers are also suitable to produce microporous hydrophobic membranes for MD by different processes. An overview of the polymers successfully used in membrane synthesis and the achieved contact angles is given in Table 5.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of the membranes synthesized from the novel polymer types in MD is given in Table 6. In general lower contact angles are obtained compared to PTFE membranes (>130 ° ). Moreover, relatively low membrane permeability is obtained compared to the commercially available membranes. Only the PTFE-co-CTFE shows membrane permeability above 200 kg h � 1 m � 2 bar � 1 . For these polymers, the membrane synthesis methods are still in early research phase and further insights in the membrane formation process are required to improve the membrane structure for MD.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Future recommendations for membrane fabrication
texts.text: 5. Future recommendations for membrane fabrication

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Different hydrophobic microporous membranes produced for other technologies like MF are also suitable for MD. Both stretched and phase inverted membranes are commercially available for this purpose. As shown in Fig. 12, the stretched membranes show relatively high vapor permeability. Notwithstanding the recent research efforts in membrane engineering, currently the stretched membranes show outstanding and robust performance in MD. An important aspect is the ability for defect-free large scale production in a cost-effective way. As a drawback, these membranes compact easily, which results in performance reduction at pilot scale. Additionally, the excellent hydrophobicity of PTFE is a challenge for the production of leakage free modules. In contrast, the PE shows a quick decrease of salt retention due to the lower hydrophobicity in more extreme applications, but is easier for module production. These drawbacks of the standard PTFE and PE membranes open up perspectives for membrane improvements.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Conclusions
texts.text: 6. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first part of this review gave an overview on the basic principle of MD and how to evaluate MD membranes. Comparison of membrane fluxes between articles is difficult due to the large variety in process conditions applied at lab scale. The use of the bulk vapor permeability can compensate for this, but is - to a minor extend - affected by process conditions itself. Another important aspect for evaluation of the membrane is to choose proper process conditions at lab scale, which can be translated to pilot scale results. At pilot scale, lower driving force and lower flowrates are applied compared to lab scale experiments. Therefore, important effects of salinity, membrane thermal conductivity and energy efficiency of the membrane are strongly underestimated at lab scale.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The second part of this review described and evaluates the performance of different membrane production methods for MD.
texts.text: The second part of this review described and evaluates the performance of different membrane production methods for MD.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Stretched membranes show good MD performance, but have the drawback of compressibility. PTFE is also difficult to glue, whereas PE shows less hydrophobicity. Therefore, design of novel membranes using the phase inversion technique using membrane modification methods to tune the surface chemistry might be a promising method to improve the membrane performance in MD. Also the use of novel and more versatile polymers is a possible route. Electrospinning can result in a very high porosity, but requires sufficient control over the pore size. In general, the production of large amounts of membranes without defects and at a low cost is crucial for the process.
